content.text: Password Hash Synchronization (PHS) is the simplest and most resilient authentication method in a hybrid identity deployment. Entra Connect (or Cloud Sync) reads the NTLM password hash from Active Directory and derives a second hash using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 with 1000 iterations and a random 10-byte salt. This derived value — not the original NTLM hash — is transmitted over TLS to Entra ID and stored there.

At sign-in time, Entra ID derives the same hash from the submitted password and compares it to the stored value. The on-premises Active Directory and the sync engine are not contacted during authentication. This means PHS sign-ins succeed even if every on-premises server is offline.

attrs.alt: PHS hash derivation chain: AD password → NTLM hash → PBKDF2 salted hash → Entra ID storage and verification

content.text: What PHS Does and Does Not Sync

PHS synchronizes a derived credential that is strictly one-way. The design ensures Entra ID can verify passwords without ever having access to the original credential or the raw NTLM hash. It also synchronizes password expiry and account lockout signals so that account state changes in AD are reflected in Entra ID within 2 minutes.
The only credential value sent to Entra ID
Never leaves the sync engine memory
Only the PBKDF2 derivative is transmitted
LM hashes are disabled in modern AD and never synced
Kerberos is not used for cloud authentication
PasswordPolicies attribute updated; SSPR respects it
Synced within ~2 minutes; affects cloud sign-in
AccountEnabled state in Entra updated on next sync cycle
These are stored in Entra ID directly, not in AD

The PBKDF2 derivative in Entra ID is computed from the NTLM hash, not the plaintext. If an attacker obtained the Entra-stored value, they could not use it directly for pass-the-hash attacks against on-premises resources because it is not the NTLM hash — it is a further transformed derivative.

PHS is enabled during the Entra Connect installation wizard or can be enabled post-installation. It can also be turned on selectively per AD connector. Once enabled, the initial sync pushes all existing password hashes, and subsequent changes are pushed within 2 minutes of the password change event in AD.

# Enable PHS on an existing Entra Connect installation
Import-Module ADSync

# List AD connectors
Get-ADSyncConnector | Where-Object { $_.Type -eq 'AD' } | Select-Object Name, Identifier

# Enable password hash sync for a specific connector
Set-ADSyncPasswordSyncConfiguration `
    -SourceConnector 'contoso.com' `
    -TargetConnector 'Entra ID Connector' `
    -Enable $true

# Restart the ADSync service to activate
Restart-Service ADSync

# Trigger an initial password hash sync (pushes all existing hashes)
Invoke-ADSyncRunProfile -ConnectorName 'contoso.com' -RunProfileName 'Full Import'
Invoke-ADSyncRunProfile -ConnectorName 'contoso.com' -RunProfileName 'Full Synchronization'
Invoke-ADSyncRunProfile -ConnectorName 'Entra ID Connector' -RunProfileName 'Export'

# Verify PHS is active: check the connector run history
Get-ADSyncConnectorRunStatus | Select-Object ConnectorName, RunProfileName, Result |
    Where-Object { $_.RunProfileName -like '*Password*' }

# Check that a specific user's hash reached Entra ID
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'User.Read.All'
Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq 'alice@contoso.com'" |
    Select-Object DisplayName, OnPremisesSyncEnabled, OnPremisesLastSyncDateTime,
        LastPasswordChangeDateTime

When a user changes their password in Active Directory, the domain controller writes the new NTLM hash to the unicodePwd attribute. Entra Connect monitors this attribute change via the DirSync replication protocol. The new hash derivative is computed on the Entra Connect server and transmitted to Entra ID, typically within 2 minutes of the AD change. Entra ID tokens obtained with the old password remain valid until they expire (access tokens: ~1 hour; refresh tokens: ~24 hours unless revoked).

# Monitor password sync events on the Entra Connect server
# Event log: Application, Source: Directory Synchronization
Get-EventLog -LogName Application -Source 'Directory Synchronization' -Newest 50 |
    Where-Object { $_.Message -match 'password' } |
    Select-Object TimeGenerated, EventID, EntryType, Message | Format-List

# Key event IDs for PHS:
# 656: Password change detected for user
# 657: Password hash sync completed successfully
# 658: Password hash sync failed (check details)

# Force immediate password hash re-sync for a specific user
# (useful after a password reset that didn't propagate)
$userDN = 'CN=Alice Smith,OU=Employees,DC=contoso,DC=com'

# Option 1: Reset and set the same password in AD (triggers a password write event)
# Only use in troubleshooting; requires knowing current password

# Option 2: Force a full password sync for the user's DN
$csObj = Get-ADSyncCSObject -ConnectorName 'contoso.com' -DistinguishedName $userDN
if ($csObj) {
    # Check last password sync time from connector space
    $csObj.Attributes | Where-Object Name -eq 'unicodePwd' |
        Select-Object Name, LastImportDateTime
} else {
    Write-Warning "User not found in connector space — check OU filter"
}

# After a password change, revoke existing Entra refresh tokens immediately
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'User.ReadWrite.All'
$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq 'alice@contoso.com'").Id
Revoke-MgUserSignInSession -UserId $userId
Write-Output "Refresh tokens revoked — next sign-in will use new password"

Microsoft's Identity Protection service cross-references PHS-synced credentials against known breach databases. If a hash derivative matches a credential appearing in a known breach, Identity Protection raises a 'Leaked Credentials' risk event for that user. This detection is only possible because PHS gives Entra ID a credential to compare — users authenticating via PTA or federation without PHS cannot benefit from this check.

# Check for leaked credential risk events via Identity Protection (requires P2)
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'IdentityRiskyUser.Read.All'

# List users with leaked credential detections
Get-MgRiskyUser -Filter "riskState eq 'atRisk'" -All |
    Select-Object UserDisplayName, UserPrincipalName, RiskLevel,
        RiskState, RiskLastUpdatedDateTime |
    Format-Table -AutoSize

# Get risk detections of type 'leakedCredentials'
Get-MgRiskDetection -Filter "riskEventType eq 'leakedCredentials'" -Top 20 |
    Select-Object UserDisplayName, UserPrincipalName, RiskLevel,
        DetectedDateTime, AdditionalInfo | Format-Table

# Remediate a user with leaked credentials
# Step 1: Force password reset
$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq 'compromised@contoso.com'").Id
Update-MgUser -UserId $userId -PasswordProfile @{
    ForceChangePasswordNextSignIn = $true
    Password = 'TemporaryP@ss2024!'
}

# Step 2: Revoke all sessions
Revoke-MgUserSignInSession -UserId $userId

# Step 3: Mark the user risk as remediated
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/dismiss' `
    -Body (@{ userIds = @($userId) } | ConvertTo-Json)
PHS vs PTA vs Federation: Resilience Comparison

The choice of authentication method in hybrid identity has direct implications for availability and resilience. PHS provides the best fault isolation because the cloud authentication path has zero dependency on on-premises infrastructure after the initial hash sync. PTA and Federation both require on-premises components to be reachable at sign-in time.
Sync in ~2 min; old tokens valid until expiry
Instant (validates against AD in real time)
Instant (validates against AD via AD FS)
AD FS lockout + Entra Smart Lockout

Microsoft recommends enabling PHS even when using PTA or Federation as a fallback authentication method. If on-premises infrastructure becomes unavailable, you can switch to PHS immediately from the Entra portal without any re-configuration of existing tokens or user objects.

Staged rollout lets you pilot PHS (or other authentication methods) for a subset of users without changing the domain-level federation settings. Users in the staged rollout group authenticate via PHS while all other users continue using PTA or AD FS. This lets you validate PHS behavior before committing to a full cutover.

# Enable staged rollout for PHS
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.ReadWrite.FeatureRollout','Group.Read.All'

# Create a staged rollout policy for PHS
$policy = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/featureRolloutPolicies' `
    -Body (@{
        displayName            = 'PHS Staged Rollout Pilot'
        feature                = 'passwordHashSync'
        isEnabled              = $true
        isAppliedToOrganization = $false  # group-scoped, not tenant-wide
    } | ConvertTo-Json)

$policyId = $policy.id

# Add a pilot group to the staged rollout policy
$pilotGroupId = (Get-MgGroup -Filter "displayName eq 'PHS-Pilot'").Id
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/featureRolloutPolicies/$policyId/appliesTo/`$ref" `
    -Body (@{ '@odata.id' = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directoryObjects/$pilotGroupId" } | ConvertTo-Json)

# List groups in the staged rollout policy
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/featureRolloutPolicies/$policyId/appliesTo" |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty value | Select-Object displayName, id

# Roll out to all users (remove group scope and set isAppliedToOrganization)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/featureRolloutPolicies/$policyId" `
    -Body (@{ isAppliedToOrganization = $true } | ConvertTo-Json)

# Disable staged rollout (revert to previous auth method for the group)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/directory/featureRolloutPolicies/$policyId" `
    -Body (@{ isEnabled = $false } | ConvertTo-Json)

Entra ID's Smart Lockout protects accounts from brute-force attacks. It tracks failed sign-in attempts per account and applies a lockout after a configurable threshold. With PHS, Smart Lockout operates in Entra ID independently of the on-premises AD lockout policy. If a user is locked out in Entra ID, they cannot sign in via cloud apps even if their AD account is unlocked — and vice versa.

# View and configure Smart Lockout settings
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.ReadWrite.Authorization'

# Read current Smart Lockout configuration
$authPolicy = Get-MgPolicyAuthorizationPolicy
$authPolicy | Select-Object DefaultUserRolePermissions |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty DefaultUserRolePermissions

# Smart Lockout thresholds are set in the Entra portal:
# Security > Authentication Methods > Password Protection
# Default: lockout threshold = 10 failed attempts, lockout duration = 60 seconds

# Check if a user is currently locked out in Entra ID
$upn = 'alice@contoso.com'
Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq '$upn'" |
    Select-Object DisplayName, AccountEnabled,
        @{N='SignInBlocked'; E={$_.BlockCredential}}

# Check sign-in logs for lockout events
$since = (Get-Date).AddHours(-4).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn `
    -Filter "userPrincipalName eq '$upn' and status/errorCode eq 50053 and createdDateTime ge $since" `
    -Top 10 |
    Select-Object CreatedDateTime, IpAddress,
        @{N='ErrorCode';   E={$_.Status.ErrorCode}},
        @{N='Reason';      E={$_.Status.FailureReason}}
# AADSTS50053 = account is locked (IdsLocked)

# Smart Lockout and AD lockout are INDEPENDENT
# A user locked in Entra ID (cloud lockout) can still authenticate to on-prem resources
# A user locked in AD will have that state synced to Entra via PHS within ~2 min

# To avoid dual lockout confusion, align thresholds:
# Set Entra Smart Lockout threshold LOWER than AD Fine-Grained Password Policy threshold
# This ensures Entra blocks spray attacks before they reach the AD lockout count

PHS authentication is subject to all Conditional Access policies, including MFA, compliant device, and sign-in risk conditions. Because authentication occurs fully in Entra ID, all CA controls apply at the moment of token issuance. This is a significant advantage over pass-through authentication, where CA policies are still enforced but the credential validation adds a round-trip to on-premises.

# Require MFA for all PHS-authenticated users signing into sensitive apps
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Policy.ReadWrite.ConditionalAccess'

# Create a CA policy that targets PHS users
# (All hybrid-synced users in PHS mode authenticate via Entra ID cloud)
$caPolicy = @{
    displayName = 'Require MFA for Cloud Apps - PHS Users'
    state       = 'enabled'
    conditions  = @{
        users = @{
            includeUsers  = @('All')
            excludeGroups = @('<break-glass-group-id>')
        }
        applications = @{
            includeApplications = @('All')
            excludeApplications = @('<excluded-app-id>')
        }
        # Target only risky sign-ins to reduce MFA fatigue
        signInRiskLevels = @('medium', 'high')
    }
    grantControls = @{
        operator        = 'OR'
        builtInControls = @('mfa')
    }
}
New-MgIdentityConditionalAccessPolicy -BodyParameter $caPolicy

# Monitor CA policy impact on PHS sign-ins
$since = (Get-Date).AddDays(-7).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn `
    -Filter "createdDateTime ge $since and conditionalAccessStatus eq 'failure'" `
    -Top 50 |
    Select-Object CreatedDateTime, UserPrincipalName, AppDisplayName,
        @{N='CAStatus'; E={$_.ConditionalAccessStatus}},
        @{N='CAPolicies'; E={($_.AppliedConditionalAccessPolicies | Where-Object Result -ne 'notApplied').DisplayName -join ', '}} |
    Format-Table

From a compliance perspective, PHS raises questions about data residency and credential storage. The PBKDF2 derivative is stored in the Entra ID tenant, subject to Microsoft's data residency commitments for the tenant's geography. The processing (hash derivation) occurs on the Entra Connect server on-premises before transmission. Audit evidence for PHS should document the derivation algorithm, the transmission encryption, and the absence of plaintext or NTLM hash transmission.

# Generate a PHS audit report for compliance review
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AuditLog.Read.All','User.Read.All','Organization.Read.All'

# 1. Confirm the tenant has PHS enabled
$org = Get-MgOrganization
Write-Output "Tenant: $($org.DisplayName)  ($($org.Id))"
Write-Output "OnPremisesSyncEnabled: $($org.OnPremisesSyncEnabled)"

# 2. List all on-premises synced users and their last sync time
$syncedUsers = Get-MgUser `
    -Filter 'onPremisesSyncEnabled eq true' `
    -Select 'displayName,userPrincipalName,onPremisesSyncEnabled,onPremisesLastSyncDateTime,lastPasswordChangeDateTime' `
    -All
Write-Output "Total synced users: $($syncedUsers.Count)"

# 3. Identify users whose password hash may be stale
#    (no sync in more than 30 minutes suggests a sync issue)
$threshold = (Get-Date).AddMinutes(-30)
$stale = $syncedUsers | Where-Object {
    $_.OnPremisesLastSyncDateTime -and $_.OnPremisesLastSyncDateTime -lt $threshold
}
if ($stale) {
    Write-Warning "Users with stale sync (>30 min):"
    $stale | Select-Object DisplayName, UserPrincipalName, OnPremisesLastSyncDateTime
} else {
    Write-Output 'All users synced within 30 minutes — PHS is healthy'
}

# 4. Confirm sync service account permissions (on-prem)
Import-Module ActiveDirectory
$syncAccount = 'CONTOSO\ADSyncAccount'
$domain = Get-ADDomain
(Get-Acl "AD:\$($domain.DistinguishedName)").Access |
    Where-Object { $_.IdentityReference -eq $syncAccount } |
    Select-Object ActiveDirectoryRights, IdentityReference, IsInherited

Password Hash Synchronization is the simplest, most resilient hybrid authentication method. It eliminates on-premises authentication dependencies at sign-in time, enables leaked credential detection, and provides a fallback path for PTA and federation environments. The hash derivation chain ensures Entra ID never stores the raw NTLM hash or plaintext password.
PHS stores only a PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 derivative of the NTLM hash — not the NTLM hash itself
Sign-in with PHS has zero on-premises dependency; it succeeds even during complete on-prem outages
Password changes propagate to Entra ID within ~2 minutes; revoke refresh tokens immediately after forced resets
Leaked credential detection in Identity Protection requires PHS — PTA and Federation cannot enable this feature
Enable PHS as a fallback even when using PTA or AD FS — it adds resilience with no user impact
Staged rollout lets you pilot PHS for a group before converting the domain-level authentication method
Account locked-out and disabled states sync to Entra ID alongside the credential hash
